unit.name: Autograd and gradient accumulation
unit.description: Explains PyTorch autograd, requires_grad, backward passes, gradient storage in .grad, and why gradients accumulate unless explicitly zeroed.
unit.summary: The segment introduces autograd as PyTorch's automatic differentiation system: when the forward pass defines computations over tensors, PyTorch tracks dependencies so that a later backward pass can apply the chain rule and cache gradients needed for optimization [ts=1623s] [ts=1688s]. A tensor with requires_grad participates in gradient tracking, and the instructor motivates disabling gradients at inference time because they add computation when no parameter updates are needed [ts=1706s] [ts=1745s]. The core example defines y = 3x^2 for x = 2, calls backward, and explains that x.grad becomes 12 because dy/dx = 6x and x=2 [ts=1779s] [ts=1805s]. Repeating a backward pass on another expression with the same dependency raises x.grad to 24, which is used to show that PyTorch accumulates rather than overwrites gradients by default [ts=1822s] [ts=1868s]. The instructor then connects this to minibatch training: accumulated gradients are desirable across examples before a parameter update, but they must be reset between training iterations so previous batches do not contaminate later updates [ts=1881s] [ts=2049s].

key_points.text: Autograd automatically applies the chain rule over the computation graph and caches gradients for optimization.
requires_grad controls whether PyTorch stores gradients for a tensor, and gradients can be disabled at inference time to avoid unnecessary computation.
For y = 3x^2 with x = 2, calling backward produces x.grad = 12.
Calling backward multiple times on quantities depending on the same tensor accumulates gradients instead of overwriting them.
Training loops must zero gradients between iterations to prevent previous updates from being double-counted.

A tensor with requires_grad participates in gradient tracking, and the instructor motivates disabling gradients at inference time because they add computation when no parameter updates are needed [ts=1706s] [ts=1745s]. The core example defines y = 3x^2 for x = 2, calls backward, and explains that x.grad becomes 12 because dy/dx = 6x and x=2 [ts=1779s] [ts=1805s]. Repeating a backward pass on another expression with the same dependency raises x.grad to 24, which is used to show that PyTorch accumulates rather than overwrites gradients by default [ts=1822s] [ts=1868s]. The instructor then connects this to minibatch training: accumulated gradients are desirable across examples before a parameter update, but they must be reset between training iterations so previous batches do not contaminate later updates [ts=1881s] [ts=2049s].

kp_catalog.name: Computational graphs and automatic differentiation
kp_catalog.description: General reverse-mode autodiff runs forward in topological order on a DAG and backward in reverse topological order, which is how modern frameworks implement backpropagation. A neural network can be understood as a directed acyclic graph of operations whose forward pass computes values and whose backward pass computes gradients.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because computational graphs and automatic differentiation formalizes how general reverse-mode autodiff runs forward in topological order on a DAG and backward in reverse topological order, which is how modern frameworks implement backpropagation, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context while roles split between gateway and supporting and importance labels split between critical and high.

kp_catalog.name: Gradient accumulation and zero_grad
kp_catalog.description: PyTorch accumulates gradients across backward passes, so training code must explicitly zero them before each new optimization step.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because gradient accumulation and zero_grad clarifies pyTorch accumulates gradients across backward passes, so training code must explicitly zero them before each new optimization step, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
